figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic diagram of the difference between Spike-NVPT and traditional VPT methods. The prompt generated by SpikeNVPT is in the form of binary spikes.

Figure 5: The trade-off result between average performances and parameter scales. The scatter plot size represents the standard deviation of results across both clean and noisy test sets, with specific values documented in the legend.

Table 1: Performance comparison across different datasets and noise types. Bold font and underline denote the best and the second-best performance, respectively. Subsequent tables use the same markup. The average column indicates the mean value across all seven datasets.
Table 2: Ablation experimental results on CIFAR-100.
Table 8: Performance comparison of different methods on the DTD dataset with varying noise.
Table 9: Performance comparison of different methods on the RESISC45 dataset with varying noise.
Table 10: Performance comparison of different methods on the DMLab dataset with varying noise.
Figure 6: The average accuracies at the texture scene. The shaded area indicates the accuracy fluctuation across the two datasets in this scene.
Figure 7: The similarities between the embeddings of clean and noised data at the 2nd layer and the last layer.
Table 4: Performance comparison of different methods on the CIFAR-100 dataset with varying noise.
Table 5: Performance comparison of different methods on the Caltech-101 dataset with varying noise.
Table 4–10 record the complete test results of Spike-NVPT and other baseline methods across seven datasets.

Figure 2: The overall architecture of Spike-NVPT. It consists of two main components: (1) Signal Filtering Layer. Spiking neurons in this layer filter the important information from the initial continuous prompt; and (2) Spike Discretization Unit. The extracted information is further translated into spike form to improve noise resistance, and sparse features are utilized to provide regular constraints on the classification header.
Figure 4: Images of CIFAR-100, DTD, Pets, and DMLab with varying noise intensities.
